text.title: Usability Testing Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every usability-testing application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Tasks MUST be scenario-based (situation + goal); MUST NOT name UI elements or describe navigation steps.
rule.rationale: Instruction-based tasks invalidate the observation because the participant is following directions, not behaving as a user.

rule.statement: Each task MUST carry a defined success criterion (observable end-state) before the session begins.
rule.rationale: Without a pre-defined criterion success becomes post-hoc judgement and is irreproducible.

rule.statement: Facilitators MUST NOT help participants during tasks, even when participants are clearly stuck.
rule.rationale: Every moment of struggle is data; helping invalidates the observation and corrupts the rest of the session.

rule.statement: Session notes MUST separate observation (what happened) from inference (why). Mixing the two is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Mixed notes prevent re-interpretation and bake in early hypotheses.

rule.statement: Every finding MUST carry a severity 1-4. Critical (1) = task-blocked AND ≥60% participants; High (2) = blocked OR ≥60%; Medium (3) = 40-60%; Low (4) = <40%. Severity is computed, not opinionated.
rule.rationale: Rubric-driven severity prevents debate; teams can argue about fixes but not about severity.

rule.statement: Pattern detection requires ≥5 participants PER SEGMENT, not 5 total. Cross-segment findings without sufficient N per segment are forbidden.
rule.rationale: 5-per-segment is the practical saturation threshold; 5-total smears segment differences.

rule.statement: Critical (severity-1) findings MUST be reviewed by a human researcher before reaching the backlog.
rule.rationale: Stakes are too high for unreviewed AI classification on blockers.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
